project.title: The schoolAIR Project
project.subtitle: A comprehensive educational project that combines hands-on STEM learning with real-world environmental monitoring. There is a purpose beyond education: Our children might be exposed to unhealthy air. This project gives schools and students real-time data about the local area — not just what governments publish broadly.

educationalExcellence.title: Educational Excellence
educationalExcellence.description: Integrate IoT, environmental science, and data analysis into your curriculum through practical, hands-on learning experiences.

communityHealth.description: Monitor air quality to protect community health with real-time alerting when indoor or outdoor indicators reach unhealthy levels.

science.description: Meteorology, environmental science, physics, and chemistry through hands-on experimentation and real data analysis.

mathematics.description: Data analysis, statistics, graphing, and pattern recognition using real environmental datasets.

components.title: Technology and Components
components.subtitle: Everything you need to build your air quality monitoring station
components.introText: The goal of the SchoolAIR project is to support all the most popular educational microcontrollers, such as Arduino, m5stack and others. Student volunteers constantly port the base software to new platforms. Communications between the microcontroller and sensors are done via standard protocols like I2C, bluetooth and Wifi. This keeps costs to a minimum and creates an open system. SchoolAIR is for students and by students!

dashboardConfig.title: Dashboard Configuration
dashboardConfig.intro: Configure alert thresholds and automated actions for your school's air quality monitoring system. When sensor readings exceed these thresholds, the system will automatically trigger the specified actions.

hero.bfisTitle: Help every Area of BFIS have Clean Air
hero.genericTitle: Protect a Classroom with Clean Air
hero.bfisDescription: The SchoolAIR team has experimented widely with the latest tech components and has been able to greatly lower the cost of an air quality unit. The home build kit includes all components and step-by-step instructions; it does not include housing. You’ll proudly deliver a working unit to your school for installation — or use it at home! Each unit forever protects a classroom by letting teachers know when it’s time to ventilate — or shut a window due to poor outside air quality.
hero.genericDescription: The SchoolAIR team has experimented widely with the latest tech components and has been able to greatly lower the cost of an air quality unit. Each unit forever protects a classroom by letting teachers know when it’s time to ventilate — or shut a window due to poor outside air quality. Sponsor a unit for any school in the world.
hero.videoCaption: Stuffy rooms quickly diminish cognitive performance

progress.title: BFIS Classrooms/Play Fields Protected
progress.plaqueNote: Classrooms with air quality units will proudly exhibit a plaque: This Classroom Protected thanks to Jane & Jennifer Ramirez

cards.title: Sponsor a Unit
cards.subtitle: One-time sponsorship — each unit protects a zone forever. Housing not included — free digital plans and 3D model files provided. Data is uploaded to the SchoolAir global database. Privacy, scalability and availability to researchers and clean air advocates is professionally managed.

interior.title: Interior Unit
interior.description: Guards classrooms from stuffy or unhealthy air by monitoring CO₂ levels and ventilation. USB plug-and-play — simple to set up and use.

exterior.title: Exterior Unit
exterior.description: Full air quality monitoring station measuring fine dust (PM2.5), weather and more. Long-range Wi-Fi hub connectivity for outdoor locations.

schoolWebsite.title: Every school gets its own website
schoolWebsite.description: Each participating school receives its own branded SchoolAir portal to showcase its health and sustainability efforts, control access to its data, and enable trend analysis projects and advocacy.

patron.title: Become a SchoolAir Advocate — Sponsor our Passionate Project Team!
patron.subtitle: Your regular contribution will allow our developers, researchers and product installers to help more and more schools watch over the health of their students. We plan to do more than just monitor — our phase two will start bringing potential solutions. Help us keep going!

dashboard.liveCaption: Live sensor data from BFIS monitoring units — powered by

pm25.title: PM2.5 (Fine Particles)
pm25.text:
These tiny particles reach deep into the lungs and bloodstream. Can trigger asthma attacks and increase cardiovascular risk.

WHO Guidelines:
• 0-15 µg/m³: Good
• >35 µg/m³: Unhealthy

pm10.title: PM1.0 (Ultrafine Particles)
pm10.text: Derived from combustion (engines, smoke). No official limit, but high levels are linked to systemic inflammation as they bypass lung barriers entirely.

pm100.title: PM10.0 (Coarse Particles)
pm100.text:
Mostly dust, pollen, and mold. Causes throat irritation, sneezing, and upper respiratory discomfort.

WHO Guidelines:
• 0-45 µg/m³: Acceptable
• >45 µg/m³: High

co2.title: CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)
co2.text:
• 400-1000 ppm: Good
• 1000-2000 ppm: Drowsiness and poor concentration
• >2000 ppm: Headaches and stuffiness. May even affect your test scores.

temp.title: Temperature
temp.text: Ideal indoor range: 20-24°C (68-75°F). Extreme temperatures affect productivity and can exacerbate existing respiratory conditions.

humidity.title: Humidity
humidity.text:
Ideal range: 30-50%.
High humidity (>60%) promotes mold growth and dust mites.
Low humidity (<30%) causes dry skin and increases viral transmission.

co.title: Carbon Monoxide (CO)
co.text: A colorless, odorless toxic gas. High levels indicate faulty heating systems or vehicle exhaust infiltration. Can cause dizziness and, at high levels, is fatal.

no2.title: Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)
no2.text: Primarily from traffic exhaust. Known to inflame the airways and is a major trigger for chronic bronchitis and asthma exacerbations.

voc.title: VOC Index (Volatile Organic Compounds)
voc.text: Gasses emitted from paints, markers, and new furniture. High levels cause "Sick Building Syndrome" — headaches, nausea, and eye irritation.

howItWorks.subtitle: A simple 4-step process to implement environmental monitoring in your school

order.title: 1. Schools Order
order.description: Get complete kits with all necessary components, sensors, and hardware, or just download the detailed plans and source components yourself. Both options include comprehensive setup guides and educational materials.

build.title: 2. Students Build
build.description: Students assemble the air quality monitoring station at school through hands-on workshops, learning electronics, programming, and environmental science. Our guides support both classroom and remote learning scenarios.

deploy.title: 3. Stations Deployed
deploy.description: Completed stations are installed around the school campus to monitor air quality, temperature, and humidity in real-time, protecting the school community with timely alerts and actionable data.

collect.title: 4. Data Collected
collect.description: Environmental data is automatically stored in global databases via our secure API, enabling students to analyze trends, contribute to climate research, and compare data with schools worldwide.

schools.title: School Network Directory
